clinicalFeed.content: S: Dr. Smith, this is the nurse calling about the 22-year-old male in room 402, who is 6 hours post-op from a left tibia ORIF. He is reporting new, severe, and unrelenting 10/10 pain in his left leg. B: His surgery was at 1800. He received 4 mg of IV morphine at 2300 without any relief. A: His left foot is cool and pale with a faint pedal pulse. Capillary refill is over 3 seconds, and he has excruciating pain with passive toe extension. R: I am concerned for acute compartment syndrome and need you to come evaluate him immediately for a possible fasciotomy.

tabs.title: Notes
tabs.content:
Postoperative Note, 1800:
Patient underwent successful open reduction and internal fixation of the left tibia. A long leg cast was applied for immobilization. Patient transferred to PACU in stable condition.

tabs.title: Medications
tabs.content:
Morphine 4 mg IV every 4 hours PRN for severe pain.
Last dose administered at 2300.

tabs.title: Vitals
tabs.content:
Time: 0000
Temp: 37.2 C (99.0 F)
HR: 112 bpm
RR: 20 breaths/min
BP: 142/88 mmHg
SpO2: 98% on room air

stem: The nurse is caring for a 22-year-old male 6 hours after an open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) of the left tibia. The client reports severe, unrelenting pain unrelieved by morphine, and his foot is cool with a faint pulse. Based on the assessment findings, complete the bowtie to guide the client's care.

potentialConditions.hoverRationale: This is a chronic condition related to atherosclerosis. The client's acute onset of symptoms following trauma and surgery makes an acute process like compartment syndrome far more likely.

Increased pressure within a fascial compartment compresses blood vessels and nerves, leading to ischemia and necrosis. The client's pain out of proportion to injury, pallor, and paresthesia are classic signs of this process.
Cellulitis is a bacterial skin infection. While it causes pain and redness, it would not typically cause the acute, severe neurovascular compromise seen in this client.
A DVT would present with unilateral edema, warmth, and calf tenderness. The client's symptoms of coolness, pallor, and severe pain are more indicative of arterial, not venous, compromise.

actions.hoverRationale: Immediate notification is critical as compartment syndrome is a surgical emergency requiring a fasciotomy. Delaying this action can lead to permanent neuromuscular damage or limb loss due to prolonged tissue ischemia.

actions.text: Position the limb at the level of the heart
actions.hoverRationale: Positioning the limb at heart level is a supportive measure, not a priority action. It does not resolve the underlying cause and should not delay the critical actions of relieving pressure and notifying the surgeon.

actions.text: Apply ice packs and elevate the limb above the heart
actions.hoverRationale: Elevation above the heart and applying ice are contraindicated. These actions decrease arterial inflow and cause vasoconstriction, respectively, which will worsen the already compromised tissue perfusion and exacerbate ischemic injury.

actions.text: Bivalve or remove the restrictive cast/dressing
actions.hoverRationale: This is a priority intervention to relieve external compression on the limb. Reducing pressure within the fascial compartment is critical to restore blood flow and prevent irreversible ischemic damage to muscles and nerves.

actions.text: Administer an additional dose of morphine
actions.hoverRationale: The client's pain is due to ischemia, not just surgical trauma. Additional opioids will be ineffective and may mask worsening symptoms, delaying definitive treatment for compartment syndrome.

parameters.hoverRationale: Serial assessment of the 6 Ps (pain, pallor, pulselessness, paresthesia, paralysis, poikilothermia) is essential. Worsening findings indicate escalating intracompartmental pressure and progressive ischemic damage to nerves and muscles.

parameters.text: Pain level and response to analgesics
parameters.hoverRationale: Pain out of proportion to the injury and unrelieved by opioids is a hallmark sign of compartment syndrome. This symptom reflects severe tissue ischemia and nerve compression within the affected compartment.
Serum calcium levels are not relevant to the acute pathophysiology of compartment syndrome. This parameter does not provide data on tissue perfusion, nerve function, or intracompartmental pressure.
An elevated WBC count indicates inflammation or infection, not compartment syndrome. This parameter does not assess the primary problem of compromised neurovascular status due to increased pressure.
While a definitive diagnostic tool, obtaining this measurement is an invasive procedure performed by the provider. The nurse's priority is to monitor non-invasive clinical signs and symptoms.

rationale.correct: The client's signs—severe pain unrelieved by opioids, pallor, coolness, and a faint pulse post-ORIF—are classic indicators of acute compartment syndrome. This condition involves increased pressure within a fascial compartment, compromising circulation and nerve function. Priority actions must relieve pressure (remove cast) and secure definitive surgical intervention (notify surgeon).
rationale.incorrect: Applying ice and elevating the limb are contraindicated as they worsen ischemia. Positioning the limb at heart level is a correct supportive measure but is not a priority over relieving constriction. Monitoring WBC and calcium levels is not relevant to the immediate life-threatening condition.
clinicalPearls: Pain out of proportion to the injury is the cardinal sign. Pulselessness is a late and ominous sign indicating severe compromise. Early recognition and intervention are crucial to prevent permanent damage. Consider compartment syndrome in any patient with a cast or tight dressing who reports increasing pain.

questionTrap.howToOvercome: Remember that in compartment syndrome, the goal is to optimize arterial inflow. Elevating the limb further reduces arterial pressure, exacerbating ischemia. Keeping the limb at heart level provides the best balance of perfusion and edema control.

mnemonic.title: The 6 P's of Compartment Syndrome
mnemonic.expansion: Pain (unrelieved), Paresthesia (numbness/tingling), Pallor (pale), Pulselessness (late sign), Paralysis (inability to move), Poikilothermia (coolness).

reviewUnits.heading: Compartment Syndrome — Pathophysiology & Recognition
reviewUnits.body: Compartment syndrome occurs when pressure within a muscle compartment increases, compromising blood flow and nerve function. Key signs include pain out of proportion to the injury, pain with passive stretch, and the 6 Ps. Early recognition is crucial to prevent irreversible damage.

reviewUnits.heading: Priority Interventions for Compartment Syndrome
reviewUnits.body: The priority nursing interventions are: Notify the surgeon immediately and relieve any external pressure (e.g., bivalve cast). Monitor neurovascular status and pain level. Avoid elevation and ice.
